coredata.dc:title: High hydrogen permeability in the Nb-rich Nb–Ti–Ni alloy
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, W.
Ishikawa, K.
Aoki, K.
coredata.dc:description: The microstructure and the hydrogen permeability of the Nb-rich Nb–Ti–Ni alloy, i.e., the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy were investigated and compared with those of the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloy. The Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy consisted of a combination of the primary phase bcc- (Nb, Ti) solid solution with the eutectic phase {bcc- (Nb, Ti)+B2-TiNi}. The volume fraction of the former and the latter phases were 62 and 38vol.%, respectively. The Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy showed the higher Φ value of 3.47×10−8 (molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5) at 673K, which is 1.8 times higher than that of the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloy, which has been reported to be highest in the Nb–Ti–Ni system. The present work demonstrated that the Nb-rich Nb–Ti–Ni alloys consisting of only the primary phase bcc- (Nb, Ti) and the eutectic phase {bcc- (Nb, Ti)+B2-TiNi} are promising for the hydrogen permeation membrane.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen absorbing materials
Metal hydrides
X-ray diffraction
Diffusion
Microstructure

for the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy between 523 and 673K.

row.entry: Chemical composition of the constituting phases (mol%)

article.ce:title: High hydrogen permeability in the Nb-rich Nb–Ti–Ni alloy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science, Kitami Institute of Technology, 165 Koen-Cho, Kitami, Hokkaido 090-8507, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Kitami Institute of Technology, 165 Koen-Cho, Kitami, Hokkaido 090-8507, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The microstructure and the hydrogen permeability of the Nb-rich Nb–Ti–Ni alloy, i.e., the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy were investigated and compared with those of the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloy. The Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy consisted of a combination of the primary phase bcc- (Nb, Ti) solid solution with the eutectic phase {bcc- (Nb, Ti)+B2-TiNi}. The volume fraction of the former and the latter phases were 62 and 38vol.%, respectively. The Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy showed the higher Φ value of 3.47×10−8 (molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5) at 673K, which is 1.8 times higher than that of the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloy, which has been reported to be highest in the Nb–Ti–Ni system. The present work demonstrated that the Nb-rich Nb–Ti–Ni alloys consisting of only the primary phase bcc- (Nb, Ti) and the eutectic phase {bcc- (Nb, Ti)+B2-TiNi} are promising for the hydrogen permeation membrane.

ce:section.ce:para: Pd–Ag alloys are mainly used as a hydrogen permeation membrane for the separation and purification of hydrogen gas. However, since Pd is too expensive and scarce in resources, it is eagerly desired to develop non-palladium based and low cost hydrogen permeation alloys [1]. Although the hydrogen permeability Φ of the group 5A metals, i.e. vanadium, niobium and tantalum, is much higher than that of Pd and Pd–Ag alloys, these metals and their alloys break down by hydrogen embrittlement and can not be used as a hydrogen permeation membrane [2]. On the contrary, Hashi et al. have recently discovered that Nb-poor Nb–Ti–Ni alloys show a large resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement and the high hydrogen permeability [3,4]. They have shown that the Φ value increases with increasing Nb content, but the Nb content of the hydrogen permeable alloys is limited to below 40mol% [3,4]. For instance, Φ is measurable in the Nb39Ti31Ni30 alloy. However, Φ is immeasurable in the Nb42Ti20Ni38 and Nb43Ti34Ni23 alloys, because the former alloy is brittle in the as-cast state and the latter one is broken down by the hydrogen embrittlement [3,4]. The hydrogen permeable Nb39Ti31Ni30 alloy consists of the primary phase bcc- (Nb, Ti) and the eutectic phase {(Nb, Ti)+TiNi} phase. On the other hand, the Nb42Ti20Ni38 alloy consists of the primary phase, the TiNi and brittle Nb8Ti3Ni9 compounds, while the Nb43Ti34Ni23 alloy consists of the primary phase, the eutectic phase and the TiNi compound. These experimental results suggest that the hydrogen permeability is measurable in the alloys consisting of only the primary phase and the eutectic phase, because these alloys are strong to the hydrogen embrittlement. It is expected that the alloys on the line connecting the eutectic and the primary compositions consist of both the primary phase and the eutectic one. Chemical compositions of the eutectic phase and the primary one in the Nb39Ti31Ni30 alloy are expressed as Nb20.5Ti38.5Ni41 and Nb83Ti13Ni4, respectively. Then, the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy on the line connecting these two compositions may consist of only the primary phase and the eutectic one. In this letter, we report the microstructure and the hydrogen permeability Φ in the Nb-rich Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy.

The Nb56Ti23Ni21 and Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloys were prepared by arc melting using high purity Nb (99.9mass% purity), Ti (99.5mass% purity) and Ni (99.9mass% purity) in a purified argon atmosphere. In the present work, the alloy compositions are described in mol%. The as-cast alloys were cut into a disks of 12mm in diameter and 0.8mm in thickness with spark erosion wire-cutting machine. Both sides of the disks were polished with buff and α-alumina of 0.5μm particle size, and coated with Pd of a thickness of 190nm by the rf-sputtering machine to avoid oxidation. A microstructure of the as-cast samples was observed with a scanning electron microscope (SEM). Chemical compositions of the constituting phases were analyzed by using an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). Crystal structures of the alloys were identified with a powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD). The hydrogen permeability Φ was determined by using a hydrogen permeation measuring method, which has been described in details in the previous paper [3]. The hydrogen flux passing through the disk sample was measured by a flow meter. The hydrogen flux J is expressed as follows:
(1)
J = Φ (
P u
0.5
−
P d
0.5
) / L
where L is the thickness of the disk, and P u and P d are a hydrogen pressure at the upstream and downstream sides, respectively. The value of Φ was calculated from the slope of the straight line plotted as the product of the hydrogen flux and the thickness of the disk J × L against the difference of square root of the partial hydrogen pressures
P u
0.5
−
P d
0.5
.

Fig. 1 shows an XRD pattern of the as-cast Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy. This alloy consists of the bcc- (Nb, Ti) solid solution and the B2-TiNi compound in the same way as the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloy (not shown). The lattice parameters of the (Nb, Ti) solid solution and the TiNi compound for the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy are calculated to be 0.330 and 0.303nm, respectively.

Fig. 2 shows SEM photographs of the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy. The white phase is the primary (Nb, Ti) solid solution phase, and the dark one is the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiNi} phase. The right side photograph is the enlarged one of the eutectic phase which consists of layered (Nb, Ti) and TiNi microstructures. Thus, the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy consists of the primary phase and the eutectic one. The volume fraction of the primary and the eutectic phases for the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy are 62 and 38vol.%, respectively. On the other hand, those of the primary and the eutectic phases for the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloy are 40 and 60vol.%, respectively. Chemical compositions of the primary and the eutectic phases for the Nb56Ti23Ni21 and the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloys are tabulated in Table 1 . The chemical compositions of the primary phase and the eutectic one in the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy are expressed as Nb87Ti11Ni2 and Nb21Ti37Ni42, respectively, which are nearly the same as those of the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloy. That is, the primary phase is Nb-rich bcc-(Nb, Ti) solid solution dissolved with a small amount of Ni.

Fig. 3 shows the plot of (J × L) versus
(
P u
0.5
−
P d
0.5
)
for the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy between 523 and 673K. The linear relation coefficients R 2 are obtained by a linear regression analysis. The R 2 values are over 0.994 in the temperature range of 523–673K. Therefore, it is reasonable to calculate Φ from the slope of these straight lines.

Fig. 4 shows the value of Φ for the Nb56Ti23Ni21 and the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloys in the form of Arrhenius plot. The hydrogen permeability Φ of pure Pd obtained using the same apparatus and a same experimental procedure are also plotted for reference. The Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy shows higher Φ values than those of the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloy and Pd between 523 and 673K. The value of Φ for the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy is 3.47×10−8 (molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5) at 673K, which is 1.8 times higher than that of the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloy. Thus, the present work demonstrates that the Nb-rich Nb–Ti–Ni alloy consisting of only the primary phase (Nb, Ti) and eutectic phase {(Nb, Ti)+TiNi} shows a large resistance to hydrogen embrittlement and a high hydrogen permeability. Then, the further improvement of Φ is anticipated by increasing the amount of Nb content if the alloys consist of only the primary phase (Nb, Ti) and eutectic phase {(Nb, Ti)+TiNi}.

The Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy consists of the primary phase bcc- (Nb, Ti) solid solution and the eutectic phase {bcc- (Nb, Ti)+B2-TiNi}. The value of Φ for the Nb56Ti23Ni21 alloy is 3.47×10−8 (molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5) at 673K, which is 1.8 times higher than that of the Nb40Ti30Ni30 alloy. The present work demonstrates that the Nb-rich Nb–Ti–Ni alloy consisting of only the primary phase and the eutectic phase shows a high hydrogen permeability and resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported in part by a Grant-in-Aid for Scientific Research (B) of Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS), and by Industrial Technology Research Grant Program in ’04 from New Energy and Industrial Technology Department Organization (NEDO) of Japan.
